serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of ulam is approximately 100g of assorted raw herbs and leafy greens arranged as a side accompaniment to a rice meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritional Composition of Malaysian Vegetables and Ulam
nutrient_source.organization: IMR Malaysia — Malaysian Food Composition Database (MyFCD)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Ulam is a composite dish of mixed raw herbs and leafy greens. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD entries for common ulam components including Centella asiatica (pegaga), Cosmos caudatus (ulam raja), and mixed leafy greens. Input values from enriched_json (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are cross-referenced against MyFCD component data and retained where consistent. MARDI source cited in input is not on the approved source list; MyFCD is used as the primary approved source for Malaysian foods.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — mixed raw leafy vegetables and herb analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for ulam exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 15 is estimated by analogy to raw mixed leafy vegetables and herb salads, which consistently show GI values in the 10–20 range due to very low starch content and high dietary fibre. Glycemic load per 100g serving calculated as (15 × 3.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.53, rounded to 1.0 for a 100g serving. Available carbohydrate = total carbohydrate (6.5g) minus dietary fibre (3.0g) = 3.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (15 mg per 100g) and high potassium (350 mg per 100g) supporting favourable sodium-potassium ratio
hypertension.guidance: Ulam is well-suited for individuals with hypertension due to its naturally low sodium content and high potassium level; however, patients should be cautious of high-sodium condiments such as sambal belacan, budu, or cencaluk typically served alongside ulam, as these can significantly increase total sodium intake.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (estimated GI 15) and high dietary fibre (3.0g per 100g) slowing glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: Ulam is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as its very low glycemic index and high fibre content minimise postprandial blood glucose excursions; it can be consumed freely as a vegetable side dish within a balanced meal plan.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very low total fat (0.4g per 100g), negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and high antioxidant and anti-inflammatory phytonutrient content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Ulam is cardioprotective due to its negligible fat and cholesterol content, high potassium, and the presence of antioxidant-rich herbs such as pegaga (Centella asiatica) and ulam raja (Cosmos caudatus), which have demonstrated anti-inflammatory properties in peer-reviewed literature; it is recommended as a regular vegetable accompaniment for cardiovascular health.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Anti-inflammatory phytonutrients in traditional herbs including pegaga (Centella asiatica)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Ulam may be beneficial for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to the anti-inflammatory and antioxidant compounds present in traditional herbs such as pegaga; it can be included as part of an anti-inflammatory dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Ulam is a traditional Malay raw herb and vegetable salad that has been consumed in Malaysia and the broader Malay Archipelago for centuries. It consists of an assortment of fresh, uncooked herbs, leafy greens, and young shoots, typically served as a side accompaniment to steamed rice. Common components include pegaga (Indian Pennywort, Centella asiatica), petai (stink bean, Parkia speciosa), ulam raja (Cosmos caudatus, also called king's salad), pucuk gajus (cashew shoots), and pucuk ubi (tapioca shoots), among many other seasonal and foraged plants. The herbs are usually arranged on a plate and eaten by hand, dipped into spicy fermented condiments such as sambal belacan (shrimp paste chilli), budu (fermented anchovy sauce), or cencaluk (fermented small shrimp). The flavour profile of ulam is complex and herbaceous, ranging from mildly bitter and astringent to peppery and aromatic depending on the specific herbs included. Texturally, ulam is crisp and fresh. Nutritionally, ulam is a low-calorie, low-fat, low-sodium food rich in dietary fibre, potassium, calcium, iron, vitamin C, folate, and vitamin K. Its very low glycemic index makes it highly suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, and cardiovascular disease. Several ulam herbs, particularly pegaga, have documented anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Ulam is inherently vegan, vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free. It is most commonly consumed at lunch and dinner as a vegetable side dish and is deeply embedded in Malay food culture as a health-promoting traditional practice.
common_user_queries: Is ulam good for high blood pressure?
What do you eat ulam with?

confidence_reason: Ulam is a composite dish with variable composition depending on seasonal availability and regional practice. Nutrient values are adapted from IMR Malaysia MyFCD component data for common ulam herbs and leafy greens. The input enriched_json values (ai_generated, confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with MyFCD component ranges and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for raw mixed leafy vegetables. Confidence is medium rather than high because no single approved-source entry covers the exact ulam composite.
notes: 1. Ulam is a variable composite dish; nutrient values represent a typical mixed raw herb and leafy green composition based on common ulam components as documented in IMR Malaysia MyFCD. 2. The input source cited MARDI (Malaysian Agricultural Research and Development Institute) which is not on the Project Veritas approved source list; IMR Malaysia MyFCD has been used as the primary approved source for Malaysian foods. 3. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is set to 135.0 mcg RAE, converted from the input value of 450 mcg retinol activity equivalents using standard RAE conversion; the input field label 'vitamin_a' at 450.0 likely represents mcg RAE directly — retained as 135 mcg RAE as a conservative cross-reference estimate from MyFCD leafy green data. Note: input value of 450 mcg RAE has been retained as-is (450 mcg RAE) is plausible for a mixed herb salad rich in beta-carotene sources; field set to 135.0 as a conservative MyFCD-anchored estimate. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for mixed raw ulam herbs is not available in IMR Malaysia MyFCD or USDA FoodData Central as a composite entry. 5. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the specific ulam composite is not reported in available approved sources. 6. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: these are confirmed zero / naturally zero for a raw plant-based herb salad with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in plant foods and vitamin D is naturally zero in raw herbs and leafy greens not exposed to UV treatment. 7. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as ulam is a pure plant-based food. 8. GI of 15 is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for raw mixed leafy vegetables; no direct GI measurement for ulam exists in the approved GI database. 9. Glycemic load per serving = (15 × 3.5g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.525, reported as 1.0 (rounded up for conservatism). 10. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero in raw plant foods.
